item_scores.reasoning: 5 alternatives named: Copilot in VS Code/Visual Studio, Windsurf (Codeium), open-source stacks (Continue.dev, Cline), status quo (vanilla editor + ChatGPT tab — manual workflow), doing nothing / banning AI in the IDE. Both non-obvious alternatives (status quo, do-nothing) explicitly included.

4 attributes, each demonstrably absent from named alternatives: multi-frontier-model routing (Copilot anchored to one lab, Windsurf narrower, OSS requires wiring); codebase-aware agentic workflows native to editor (alternatives are bolt-ons or side-panels); VS Code-fork product surface (alternatives constrained by host-IDE plugin contracts); brand pull at density (named bottom-up moat). Each attribute concrete, not generic.

All 4 themes connect attributes to specific outcomes with magnitudes: model uptake in days vs. quarterly vendor release (time-to-frontier metric); 10+ file changes from one prompt instead of half-day mechanical edits (specific unit-of-work); 130%+ NDR as teams standardize (named retention metric); near-zero CAC for Business tier (cost-of-acquisition outcome).

'VP of Engineering or Engineering Manager at a 50-to-1,000-engineer venture-backed software company (fintech, AI/ML, dev tools, consumer internet) headquartered in the US, UK, or EU.' Buyer title + industry (named verticals) + size band + geography. Plus precondition: ICs already adopted bottom-up. Tightly specified.

Default category named explicitly ('AI coding assistant') with why-it-loses argument (Copilot's Microsoft distribution dominates the frame). Chosen category ('the AI-native code editor for production engineering teams — sibling to VS Code, not Copilot peer') with explicit gains (differentiation, pricing power, right buyer in eng leadership not IT, defensible moat from editor-not-plugin status) AND explicit give-ups (slower comprehension, no easy-bundling story, F500 buyer treats 'new IDE' as heavier security review).

'CIOs and CISOs at Fortune 500 banks, healthcare systems, defense contractors, and federal agencies who require FedRAMP, HIPAA, on-prem / VPC, or BYO-model.' Role + 4 named industries + specific blocking criteria. Plus secondary: free-tier hobbyists with rationale ('brand asset, not revenue segment').

Invokes ONE specific trend (model-provider multipolarity — labs trading frontier lead quarterly) with concrete rationale: in one-lab world Copilot wins via distribution; in multipolar world the editor that routes per-task wins, which is structurally what Cursor does. Plus explicit decline of generic 'AI is eating software' trend with rationale (would equally describe every named competitor).

'For engineering managers and VPs of Engineering at venture-backed software companies whose ICs have already adopted it bottom-up, Cursor is the AI-native code editor — a category sibling to VS Code, not a plugin like Copilot — that delivers fork-level agentic workflows and per-task frontier-model routing the host-IDE alternatives structurally cannot ship.' Combines all three ingredients (best-fit customer + primary value theme + competitive alternative). Specific enough that Copilot could not equally claim this (Copilot IS a plugin by this framing).
